title: Glass Balls

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: You have a 100-story building and 2 identical glass balls. There is some critical floor X (between 1 and 100) such that any ball dropped from floor X or higher BREAKS, and any ball dropped from a floor below X SURVIVES.
rules: You can drop a ball from any floor.
If a ball survives, you can pick it up and reuse it.
If a ball breaks, that ball is gone.
Worst case: minimize the maximum number of drops needed to find X with certainty.
setup.question: What is the minimum number of drops in the worst case?

learningObjectives: Recognize that the first ball is your COARSE probe; the second is the LINEAR fallback.
Use a decreasing-step strategy: each successive first-ball drop covers fewer floors.
Bound the worst case via N + (N-1) + ... + 1 = N(N+1)/2 floors covered, and solve for the smallest N.

stages.label: Why naive strategies are suboptimal
stages.prompt: Suppose you drop the first ball from every 10th floor (10, 20, 30, ...). Once it breaks, you check each floor sequentially with the second ball. What's the worst case?

options.text: 10 + 9 = 19 drops in the worst case (first ball: up to 10 drops to find the break interval; second ball: up to 9 drops within the interval).

stages.explanation: Equal-spacing 10/10/10/.../10 has worst case ~19. Can we do better? Yes -- if the first ball drops are spaced UNEQUALLY (decreasing), the worst case shrinks.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: How do you find the optimal spacing?

options.text: Series summation: if the worst case is N drops total, the first ball can be dropped from N successively-decreasing-step floors. Sum N + (N-1) + ... + 1 = N(N+1)/2 must cover all 100 floors.
Probability over the unknown floor X.

stages.explanation: If the first ball is dropped from floor N, then floor N + (N-1), then floor N + (N-1) + (N-2), and so on, then EACH successive jump is one smaller. This means: after K drops without breaking, the first ball has covered N + (N-1) + ... + (N-K+1) floors. After all N drops, we've covered N(N+1)/2 floors. That's our budget.

stages.label: Why decreasing steps?
stages.prompt: Why should the first ball's step size DECREASE after each drop that survives?

options.text: Because each drop you've already used is a drop you can't spend on the linear scan after the first ball breaks. You want to keep the total budget (first-ball drops + second-ball linear scan) constant.
Because higher floors are riskier for the ball.

stages.explanation: Total worst-case = (drops used so far on ball 1) + (linear scan within the interval after ball 1 breaks). To make this constant across all break-intervals, each successive interval (between first-ball drops) must be one floor shorter than the previous.

stages.label: Solve N(N+1)/2 >= 100
stages.prompt: What is the smallest integer N such that N(N+1)/2 >= 100?

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.prompt: What is the minimum number of drops in the worst case?

options.text: 14 drops -- with first ball dropped from floors 14, 27, 39, 50, ..., the worst-case total is 14.

stages.explanation: 14 drops minimum. First ball drops at floors 14, 14+13=27, 27+12=39, 39+11=50, 50+10=60, 60+9=69, 69+8=77, 77+7=84, 84+6=90, 90+5=95, 95+4=99 -- covers up to floor 105 (more than 100). At any break, the linear scan within the interval takes at most (step-size - 1) more drops, summing to 14 total in the worst case.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize: for B floors and 2 balls, the answer is the smallest N with N(N+1)/2 >= B. Verify on B = 50.
What changes if you have 3 balls? (Hint: the answer involves a cube-like sum.)
Why does the dual formula -- 2 balls, find N from N(N+1)/2 -- mirror so many other puzzles? What's the underlying combinatorial structure?
